coredata.dc:title: Tungsten nitride nanoparticles anchored on porous borocarbonitride as high-rate anode for lithium ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Chengxiang
Wang, Chao
Meng, Xiangwei
Li, Xingyun
Qing, Qing
Wang, Xianfen
Xue, Ruiying
Yu, Qiang
Yang, Jialei
Wang, Kuikui
Zhao, Xiaoju
Chen, Wei
Qiao, Zhen-An
Zhao, Xiu Song
coredata.dc:description: Electrode materials play a paramount role in developing high-performance lithium ion batteries (LIBs). Nanostructured electrode materials can offer unique advantages, such as high rate capability. Here, we describe a strategy for synthesizing advanced anode materials with tungsten nitride (WN) quantum dots (2–5 nm) firmly anchored on mesoporous ternary borocarbonitride (BCN). A composite sample exhibited reversible capacities of 683.4 and 366.9 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.1 and 4 A g−1, respectively, suggesting good rate capability. The electrode is also stable against cycling with only 2.7% capacity loss after 500 cycles at 1 A g−1. This high performance can be ascribed to the highly dispersed WN quantum dots, which provide a high active surface for lithium ions, and significantly shortened pathways for charge transport. In addition, the porous BCN framework facilitates transfer of the electrolyte and offers a strong binding interaction with the WN quantum dots to alleviate the volume expansion and particle agglomeration issues. Further analysis of lithium storage kinetics revealed the important role that the pseudocapacitive mechanism played in the charge storage process. This work demonstrates the great potential of tungsten-based composite as high-rate anode for high-performance LIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tungsten nitride
Borocarbonitride
Lithium ion batteries
Quantum dot

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (white circles represent some of the meso-pores) and (b) HR-TEM image of WN@BCN-900, (c, d) lattice spacing of BCN and WN for WN@BCN-900, (e) HAADF-STEM image of WN@BCN-900 and (f) size distribution of WN nanoparticles for WN@BCN-900.

(a) XRD patterns, (b) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms, (c) DFT pore size distributions, (d) XPS survey of WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800, WN@BCN-900, WN@BCN-1000, and deconvolution of (e) W 4f, (f) N 1 s XPS spectra of WN@BCN-900.

(a) CV curves of WN@BCN-900 at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1, (b) galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of WN@BCN-900 at 0.1 A g−1, (c) rate capabilities, (d) cyclic performance of WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800, WN@BCN-900, WN@BCN-1000 and Coulombic efficiency for WN@BCN-900 at 1C, (e) Nyquist plots of WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800, WN@BCN-900, WN@BCN-1000 before cycling (the inset shows the magnified view in low-medium frequency region and equivalent circuit model), and (f) rate performance compared with some WN and BCN electrodes reported in previous literatures.
Schematic illustration of charge transfer in the WN@BCN-900 electrode.

CV curves of WN@BCN-900 electrode at various sweep rates (a) 0.1 to 0.8 mV s−1, (b) 1 to 20 mV s−1, (c) relationship between the peak currents and sweep rates in logarithmic format, (d) normalized capacity versus scan rate-1/2, (e) capacitive contribution of WN@BCN-900 electrode at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1, (f) contribution ratio of the capacitive and diffusion-controlled capacity versus sweep rate.
EIS parameters obtained from fitting equivalent circuit model.

head.ce:title: Tungsten nitride nanoparticles anchored on porous borocarbonitride as high-rate anode for lithium ion batteries

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials for Energy and Environment
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Electrode materials play a paramount role in developing high-performance lithium ion batteries (LIBs). Nanostructured electrode materials can offer unique advantages, such as high rate capability. Here, we describe a strategy for synthesizing advanced anode materials with tungsten nitride (WN) quantum dots (2–5 nm) firmly anchored on mesoporous ternary borocarbonitride (BCN). A composite sample exhibited reversible capacities of 683.4 and 366.9 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.1 and 4 A g−1, respectively, suggesting good rate capability. The electrode is also stable against cycling with only 2.7% capacity loss after 500 cycles at 1 A g−1. This high performance can be ascribed to the highly dispersed WN quantum dots, which provide a high active surface for lithium ions, and significantly shortened pathways for charge transport. In addition, the porous BCN framework facilitates transfer of the electrolyte and offers a strong binding interaction with the WN quantum dots to alleviate the volume expansion and particle agglomeration issues. Further analysis of lithium storage kinetics revealed the important role that the pseudocapacitive mechanism played in the charge storage process. This work demonstrates the great potential of tungsten-based composite as high-rate anode for high-performance LIBs.

ce:section.ce:para: Rechargeable lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have become the main power source to portable electronic devices and invading into the industry of hybrid and electric vehicles [1,2]. However, the commercially used graphite anode has a number of drawbacks, such as low rate capability due to the intercalation mechanism and safety concerns due to the formation of lithium metal dendrites. As a result, recent research efforts have been devoted for the development of alternative anode materials with high capacity and rate capability, low cost and more safety [3]. For example, Deng et al. reported a composite material with mesoporous cobalt–boride nanoflakes serving as multifunctional bridges in ZnCo2O4 micro-/nanospheres [4]. This composite electrode displayed a lithium ion storage capacity of 995 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1 with a stable cycling performance. A hierarchical porous Ni-Co-Se nanoparticles embedded in 3D carbon network exhibited high rate performance with a capacity of 493 mA h g−1 at 8 A g−1 [5]. Dai et al. designed a unique ball-cactus-like composite with NiP2/Ni3Sn4 on carbon nanotubes, which exhibited a high lithium ion storage capacity of 704 mAh g−1 after 200 cycles at 0.1 A g−1 [6].

Transition metal nitrides (TMNs) have recently been demonstrated promising anode materials for LIBs owing to their high energy density, good structural stability, long cycle life and low cost [7,8]. Compared to the traditional lithium ion (Li+) intercalation mechanism in graphite, TMNs store charges via the conversion mechanism: MaNb + (b n)Li+ + (b n)e− ↔ aM + bLinN (M represents the transition metal) [7]. Tungsten nitride (WN), one of the TMNs, has gained a great deal of research interest because of its superior electronic conductivity [9–11]. Moreover, WN has tunable Li+ reaction potentials, thus can avoid the formation of lithium dendrites [2]. However, the key issues of WN are its small diffusion coefficient of Li+ and volume variation during the charge/discharge processes, leading to poor rate performance and rapid fading of capacity, respectively [12]. To overcome these problems, one of the common strategies is to use nanostructured WN electrodes to increase the specific surface area so as to expose more active sites for reaction with Li+ and shorten the transport pathway of Li+ [7,12,13]. Zhang and co-workers fabricated three-dimensional (3D) WN nanowires on carbon fiber cloth using the hydrothermal synthesis method [9]. The composite electrode exhibited good cycling ability and rate capability (a capacity of 418 mAh g−1 at 200 mA g−1). Park et al. reported mesoporous WN nanoplates as anode materials for LIBs via a template-free synthesis method [14]. The as-prepared materials delivered a high rate performance with a specific capacity of 450 mAh g−1 at 200 mA g−1.

Confining WN with porous carbon is another commonly adopted strategy. The carbon can not only buffer the strain from volume variation during the charge/discharge processes, but also improve the rate performance. Nevertheless, agglomeration of WN nanoparticles during cycling is still unavoidable [2]. Introduction of hetero-atoms such as N, B to form a ternary BCN material will form a polar surface due to the different electronegativity between B, C, and N [15]. The “lop-sided” electron distribution on the surface of BCN could offer great opportunity for the electronic interaction between BCN and the metal species [16,17]. Meanwhile, the abundant defects sites such as pores, edges, vacancies etc. in BCN material play important role to provide strong metal and support interaction (SMSI) [18–20]. Thus, the unique surface and defects structure of BCN will be appealing to bind strongly with WN when used in LIBs, which however has not been explored before.

In this paper, we report the synthesis of WN nanoparticles of 2–5 nm in diameter confined in mesoporous BCN materials. As an anode material for LIBs, a sample (designated as WN@BCN-900) exhibited specific capacities of 683.4 and 366.9 mAh g−1 at current densities of 0.1 and 4 A g−1, respectively, and an excellent cycling stability (97.3% capacity retained after 500 cycles). The excellent electrochemical performance can be attributed to the unique structure of the composite material. On the one hand, the ultra-small WN nanoparticles could provide high density of active sites for reacting with Li+ ions and enhance Li+ transport rate. Meanwhile, WN will significantly increase the electron conductivity of the composite. On the other hand, the porous BCN matrix stabilizes the WN nanoparticles against cycling. Moreover, Li+ intercalation in the layers of BCN offers additional lithium storage, making extra contribution to charge storage [21,22].

Ammonium tungstate ((NH4)10H2(W2O7)6) (99.99%, Macklin), dopamine hydrochloride (98%, Macklin), tris(hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (tris-buffer, ≥99.9%, titration, Macklin), boric acid (GR, ≥99.9%, Aladdin), urea (AR, Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co.,Ltd), N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP, Aldrich), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, Solvay S.A.) are of analytical grade and used as received.

0.15 g of ammonium tungstate was dissolved in 120 mL of deionized (DI) water with constant stirring for 1 h. Then 1.14 g of dopamine hydrochloride dissolved in 200 mL of DI water was added. After stirring for 0.5 h, tris-buffer was added to tune the pH of the solution to 8.5. The mixture was continuously stirred for 24 h and then was filtered and washed by abundant DI water. The WOx-PDA powder was obtained after drying at 60 °C overnight.

0.2 g of WOx-PDA powder, 0.4 g of boric acid and 4.8 g of urea were dissolved in 40 mL of DI water and then stirred at 80 °C to evaporate the water. The obtained WOx-PDA-H2BO3-CO(NH2)2 was thermally treated at 900 °C for 2 h at ammonia atmosphere at heating rate of 5 °C min−1 to obtain the resultant sample, which is designated as WN@BCN-900. For comparison, WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800 and WN@BCN-1000 was prepared at different calcination temperatures of 700 °C, 800 °C and 1000 °C, respectively. Another sample, designated as BCN, was also synthesized using the same procedure as that of WN@BCN-900 but without adding ammonium tungstate.

Morphologies of the samples were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) using a JEOL JSM-7800F instrument at 10 kV. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analyses were carried out with a JEOL JEM-2100. Nitrogen adsorption–desorption experiment was carried out by a Quantachrome Autosorb iQ3. The specific surface area was calculated using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method and the pore size distribution was estimated with Density Functional Theory (DFT) method. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a Rigaku Ultima IV with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 0.15418). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a Thermo Fisher ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer with an Al Kα X-ray source. Inductively Coupled Plasma (ICP) was tested on Agilent ICP-OES/730. Aberration-Corrected High-Angle Annular Dark-FieldScanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) measurements were performed on a JEM-F200 operated at 200 kV, which is equipped with two 100 mm2 SDD detectors perpendicularly installed.

The electrochemical properties of the as-prepared samples were evaluated by the CR2032 coin-type half cells. The working electrodes were prepared by coating a slurry consisting of 10 wt.% of carbon black, 80 wt.% of active materials (WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800, WN@BCN-900, WN@BCN-1000, or BCN), and 10 wt.% of binder (PVDF) in NMP solvent on a copper foil, followed by drying at 110 °C for 10 h in a vacuum oven to remove the solvent. The coated foils were then punched to disks with diameter of 10 mm and with mass loadings of 1.5 mg cm−2. All half cells were assembled in an argon-filled glove box (Mikrouna Co., Ltd., China, H2O, O2 < 0.01 ppm) using lithium foil as the counter electrode, Celgard 2600 membrane as the separator, and a solution of 1 M LiPF6 in ethylene carbonate/dimethyl carbonate (EC/DMC) with a volume ratio of 1:1 as the electrolyte.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was performed on a CHI760D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Co.) at various sweep rates in the voltage range of 0.01–3 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out using a PGSTAT302N electrochemical workstation (Metrohm Autolab Co.) in the frequency range between 0.01 Hz and 100 kHz. The galvanostatic charge/discharge tests were performed on a CT-4008 cell test instrument (Neware Co.) with the voltage range of 0.01–3 V. All the electrochemical tests were conducted at room temperature.

As schematically illustrated in Fig. 1 , WN@BCN is synthesized by a facial two step procedures. Firstly, ammonium tungstate is well chelated with dopamine during its polymerization process, leading to a WOx-PDA (polymerized dopamine) composite, which manifests a core–shell structure (Fig. S1a) with WOx well interconnected and encapsulated by nitrogen doped carbon material (Fig. S1b). It can be clearly detected from TEM images in Figs. S1c and S1d that the inner core of the composite is stacked by small WOx nanoparticles with size at around 5 nm. After the mixing of boric acid and urea, the WOx-PDA could be well linked together as the TEM shown in Fig. S1e. Then, WOx-PDA-H2BO3-CO(NH2)2 is pyrolyzed under NH3 atmosphere at high temperature (see details in the experimental part). The pyrolysis could simultaneously promote the formation of ternary BCN as well as WN materials in one pot.

As the TEM images shown in Fig. 2 a, after calcination at 900 °C, the core–shell structure of WOx-PDA is transformed into a 3D-porous structure. There are plenty of meso-pores with size at about 20–40 nm formed in the composite, which could be derived from the release of volatiles during the pyrolysis process. Both the STEM with Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX) characterization in Fig. S2 and SEM with EDX characterization in Fig. S3 demonstrate a homogeneous distribution of B, C, N, and W elements all over the framework for WN@BCN-900. From high-resolution TEM image in Fig. 2b and the HAADF-STEM image in Fig. 2e of WN@BCN-900, it can be clearly detected that there are plenty of ultra-small nanoparticles well dispersed on the nano-sheet substrate. The lattice space for the substrate is determined to be 0.34 nm corresponding to the BCN material (Fig. 2c) [17]. While the space of the crystalline lattice for the quantum dots is calculated to be 0.25 nm in accordance with the (1 1 0) planes of WN (Fig. 2d) [7]. By statically analyzing 200 granules of WN, the mean particle size is calculated to be at around 2–5 nm (Fig. 2f). Furthermore, ICP analysis shows that WN accounts for 18.7 wt% in WN@BCN-900, higher than the samples calcined at other temperatures (Table S1). From Fig. S4 we can see that all the WN@BCN-T (T = 700, 800, 900 and 1000) samples show highly porous structure. However, no obvious WN nanoparticle are observed for WN@BCN-700 (Fig.S4a and S4b). This indicates that the crystallization of WN is poor at low calcination temperature. As for WN@BCN-1000, the nanoparticles of WN are apparently aggregated (Fig. S4e and S4f), which could be induced by the higher calcination temperature. The TEM images of BCN sample in Fig. S5 shows a porous morphology similar to that of WN@BCN-900.

From the XRD characterizations for WN@BCN-T in Fig. 3 a and BCN in Fig. S6a, we can see a broad peak at around 25°, which could be ascribed to BCN material [23]. The peaks at 37.5°, 44°, 63.5°, 76.5° are corresponding to the cubic WN phase (PDF No.03-1031) [24]. There are no obvious WN peaks detected for the sample of WN@BCN-700. This could be due to the amorphous structure or poor crystalline of WN at low calcination temperature, which is in accordance with the TEM characterizations. For the sample of WN@BCN-1000, we can see the occurrence of new crystal phase of W2C. This may be formed by the promoted interaction of W with C at high calcination temperature.

From the nitrogen (N2) adsorption–desorption isotherms in Fig. 3b, it can be seen noticeable hysteresis loops in the relative pressure ranging from 0.50 to 0.95 for all the samples, suggesting the presence of meso-pores. Meanwhile, the N2 uptake at low pressure demonstrates the existence of micro-pores. The hierarchical pore structure could be supported by the DFT pore size distributions in Fig. 3c, which shows that the pore size is mainly centered at around 1.1 nm and 4.0 nm for all the samples. Table 1 shows that the specific surface area and pore volume are obviously increased from 156.4 m2 g−1 and 0.63 cc g−1 to 930.2 m2 g−1 and 1.18 cc g−1 as the calcination temperature increased from 700 to 900 °C. However, with further increase the calcination temperature to 1000 °C, the specific surface area is decreased to 584.1 m2 g−1 and the pore volume is reduced to 0.87 cc g−1. This may be due to the collapses of the pores at even higher temperature [25–27]. As comparison, BCN shows that the specific surface area and pore volume are 916.8 m2 g−1 and 1.32 cc g−1 at 900 °C (Fig. S6b), which is similar to that of WN@BCN-900.

The full XPS spectra in Fig. 3d shows the existence of W, B, C, N, O elements. It is interesting to find that the elemental composition of B, C and N is sensitive to the calcination temperature. High temperature could promote the etching of carbon with NH3 leading to a lower carbon content in BCN (Table 1). The W 4f spectrum displays four peaks at 32.4, 34.5, 35.7 and 37.7 eV in Fig. 3e, corresponding to W 4f7/2 (W-N), W 4f5/2 (W-N), W 4f7/2 (W-O) and W 4f5/2 (W-O) [29–31]. The N 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. 3f) could be fitted into 397.3, 398.1, 398.4, 399.6 and 401.1 eV, corresponding to the N-W, N-B, pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, and graphitic-N [28,29]. The B 1 s XPS spectrum (Fig. S7a) could be fitted into 190.4, 191.2 and 192.5 eV, attributed to the B-C, B-N, and B-O [23]. The C 1 s spectrum in Fig. S7b presents four peaks at 284.2, 284.5, 285.9 and 288.3 eV, which could be ascribed to C-B, C–C, C-N and C-O species in BCN [32,33]. It is reported that different atomic structure greatly influences the electrochemical performance. Particularly, pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N structure could provide more active sites for Li storage and facilitate electrons and Li ions transfer in the electrode [34,35]. We thus compared the nitrogen species of WN@BCN-T as shown in Fig. S8 and Table S2. Among all the samples, WN@BCN-900 shows the highest amount of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N content, which will contribute significantly to its LIBs capacity. To further shed light on the binding effect of BCN with WN, we carefully compared the N1s XPS spectra of BCN with WN@BCN-900. As shown in Fig. S9, it can be seen an obvious up-shift of the N1s peak for WN@BCN-900, which may be induced by the electron transfer from BCN to WN. This result may serve as an evidence for the strong electronic interaction between BCN and WN.

The electrochemical performances were all evaluated using the CR2032 coin-type half cells with WN@BCN-T as the working anode material. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) curves of WN@BCN-900 in Fig. 4 a were measured at a sweep rate of 0.1 mV s−1 in the voltage range between 0.01 and 3 V. A broad peak at around 0.5 V can be observed in the first cathodic cycle, corresponding to the reduction of WN, the decomposition of electrolyte, and the irreversible formation of solid electrolyte interface (SEI) film [36,37]. This peak disappears in the subsequent cycles, suggesting the stability of the SEI film after the first cycle [38]. In the following cathodic cycles, a new peak emerges at about 0.8 V corresponding to the electrochemical reduction of WN. For the anodic scans, a broad peak corresponding to the reversible oxidation of W appears at about 1.3 V in the first cycle and then fixes at 1.2 V at the subsequent scans, which could be due to the instability of the first cycle. The involved reversible electrochemical reactions can be described as follows [36]:
(1)
WN + 3 L
i +
+ 3
e -
↔ W + L
i 3
N

A pronounced reduction peak at 0.02 V and a broad oxidation peak at 0.25 V appear in all scans, which can be ascribed to the insertion and extraction processes of Li+ into and from the BCN material. This indicates that the reversible Li+ storage capacity for the WN@BCN-900 electrode is related to the contributions of both WN nanoparticles and BCN. In addition, the CV curves after the first cycle exhibit a high doublication, suggesting the good reversibility of the WN@BCN-900 electrode.

Fig. 4b shows the 1st, 2nd, 3rd, 5th and 10th charge/discharge profiles of WN@BCN-900 at a current density of 0.1 A g−1. The initial discharge and charge specific capacities are 940.9 and 763.9 mAh g−1, respectively. The initial Coulombic efficiency is calculated to be 81.2%, which can be attributed to the irreversible consumption of Li+ to form SEI layer [39–41]. After the first cycle, there is no obvious capacity loss in the subsequent charge/discharge profiles, suggesting the excellent reversibility of the WN@BCN-900 electrode. In accordance with the peaks appearing in CV curves, two pairs of voltage plateaus are observed during the subsequent discharge/charge processes. A sloping discharge plateau at about 0.5–1.0 V and a sloping charge plateau at about 1.0–1.6 V can be ascribed to the reduction of WN and the oxidation of W with Li3N [12]. The following sloping curves from 0.5 to 0.01 V and the initial charge slants below 0.5 V are related to the insertion and extraction of Li+ to BCN material, the reversible reduction and oxidation of WN, and some side reaction like the reversible formation and decomposition of organic polymeric film [6,9,38]. In addition, all the voltage plateaus are not obvious, which indicates a pseudocapacitive behavior may occur during the discharge/charge processes [42]. Fig. S10 shows the ex-situ XRD patterns of WN@BCN-900 electrode during the first and second discharge/charge process. At the state of discharge to 0.01 V, there present diffraction peaks of Li3N and W which can be ascribed to the reduction of WN in the WN@BCN-900 electrode. WN appears in XRD patterns when it is charged to 3.0 V, confirming the reversible transformation from W and Li3N to WN. The result of ex-situ XRD clearly reveals the redox reaction of WN@BCN-900 during the discharge/charge process [43]. Moreover, the diffraction peaks remained almost the same without any new peaks in the second discharge/charge cycle, suggesting an excellent reversibility in the WN@BCN-900 electrode.

Fig. 4c compares the rate capabilities of WN@BCN-T at different current densities ranging from 0.1 to 4 A g−1. The reversible specific capacities of 683.4, 618.3, 570.8, 504.5 and 440.3 mAh g−1 can be obtained at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, and 2 A g−1 for the WN@BCN-900 electrode. Even at a high current density of 4 A g−1, a high reversible specific capacity of 366.9 mAh g−1 is still retained. Besides, when the current density is set back to 0.1 A g−1, the WN@BCN-900 electrode delivers a specific capacity of 709.2 mAh g−1, confirming the excellent rate capability. Although the WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800, WN@BCN-1000 and BCN electrodes exhibit similar rate performances as the WN@BCN-900 electrode, their specific capacities are obvious lower. The high specific capacity for the WN@BCN-900 electrode can be ascribed to its high specific surface area, pore volume and the presence of high doping content of pyridinic-N and pyrrolic-N, which significantly increase the electrochemical sites during the lithiation/delithiation process [44,45]. From Fig. S11a, we can see that BCN electrode delivers a reversible capacity of 289.5 mAh g−1 at 0.1 A g−1, confirming the contribution of BCN for lithium storage capacity in the WN@BCN-900 composite.

Fig. 4d compares the cycling performances of the WN@BCN-T electrodes at a current density of 1 A g−1. For the WN@BCN-700, WN@BCN-800 and WN@BCN-900 electrodes, it is obvious that the specific capacities increase gradually from the 10th to the 320th cycle, which can be ascribed to the gradual access of the electrolyte into porous BCN and the activation process of the electrochemical conversion reactions between WN and W metal particles [46]. However, for the WN@BCN-1000 electrode, this phenomenon doesn’t occur. This is mainly due to the increasing size of WN particles in WN@BCN-1000 electrode, which has been demonstrated in TEM analyses. Among the four electrodes, WN@BCN-900 shows the highest reversible capacity of 529 mAh g−1 with 97.3% storage capacity retained after 500 cycles. The good cyclic stability of WN@BCN-900 can be attributed to the strong binding of WN on the BCN surface, which could efficiently alleviate the volumetric expansion of WN nanoparticles during the charge/discharge process, thus avoiding the pulverization problem [22]. Ex-situ TEM (Fig. S12) image of the WN@BCN-900 electrode after 500 cycles at 1 A g−1 further confirms that the ultra-small WN nanoparticles are well preserved after the repeated Li+ insertion and extraction without agglomeration. This result further highlights the important role of BCN to anchor WN on its surface.

From the Nyquist plots of WN@BCN-T in Fig. 4e, we can see that all plots contain a sloping line and a semicircle loop in the low and medium frequency regions, corresponding to the diffusion resistance of Li+ in the solid phase (Z w) and charge-transfer resistance at electrode/electrolyte interface (R ct), respectively [47,48]. The intercept at the real part (Z′) in the high frequency region represents the resistance of electrode and electrolyte, named as the equivalent series resistance (R s) [49,50]. Constant-phase element (CPE) is attributed to the distribution of physical properties of electrode surface [51]. The fitted values of R s, R ct and Z w using the equivalent circuit model are listed in Table 2 . It can be seen that the R ct value of the WN@BCN-900 electrode (153.5 Ω) is much lower than that of the other electrodes, suggesting that the characteristic of the porous structure and the ultra-small WN nanoparticles significantly influence the charge transfer and the related rate capability of WN@BCN-900 composite electrode. By comparing the Nyquist plots of BCN (Fig. S11b) with WN@BCN-900 (Fig. 4e), it can be seen that WN play great role in improving the electron conductivity and facilitating the charge transfer of ions, which is pivotal for the enhancement of the reversible capacity of WN@BCN-900.

The rate performance of WN@BCN-900 electrode in this work are further compared to some representative literature reported WN and BCN based material, such as layered WN nanowires [9], WN nanoplates [14], BCN nanotubes [21], BCN [52], graphene-like BCN [53]. As listed in Fig. 4 f, WN@BCN-900 electrode exhibits obviously superior electrochemical performance, which can be attributed to its unique structural features with ultra-small WN particles compactly anchored on porous BCN, as also schematically illustrated in Fig. 5 . Firstly, the ultra-small WN particles with an enlarged contact area and a shortened Li+ diffusion path could significantly increase the active sites and enable a rapid lithiation/delithiation process, thus improving the specific capacity and rate capability, respectively [22,54,55]. The hierarchical pore structure could on the one hand provide plenty of channels to facilitate a rapid transport of Li ions as well as the electrolyte and on the other hand offer sufficient spaces to alleviate the volumetric expansion of WN nanoparticles during the charge/discharge process, thus avoiding the pulverization problem. Moreover, the B, C, N well distributed polar surface can promote the wettability of the electrode material with the electrolyte and enhance the surface interaction with nanoparticles of WN to avoid its agglomeration [32,56,57].

CV measurement at different scan rate ranging from 0.1 to 20 mV s−1 were performed to investigate the lithium-storage kinetics of the WN@BCN-900 electrode by quantitatively calculating the contributions of capacitive process and diffusion process. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the CV curves exhibit similar shapes of redox peaks at the sweep rate between 0.1 and 0.8 mV s−1. However, when the sweep rate exceeds 5 mV s−1, no obvious redox peaks can be seen in the CV curves (Fig. 6b), which could be mainly attributed to the polarization of electrodes. The peak current (i) and sweep rate (v) usually obey the power-law relationship as the following equation [58,59]:
(2)
i = a
v
b
where b can be obtained from the slope of the log(i)-log(v) plot. Specifically, the b value of 0.5 implies a diffusion-controlled process, while 1 suggests the capacitive process, thus providing the information to reveal the lithium-storage mechanism [58]. As the log(i)-log(v) plot of WN@BCN-900 electrode shown in Fig. 6c, it can be seen that the b values of cathodic and anodic peaks are 0.9 and 0.85 respectively at slow sweep rates from 0.1 to 5 mV s−1, which indicates that the lithium-storage kinetics are governed by a pseudocapacitive process. At faster sweep rate ranging from 8 to 20 mV s−1, the b values decrease to 0.55 for cathodic peaks and 0.54 for anodic peaks, implying a diffusion-controlled mechanism.

Fig. 6d shows the plot of normalized capacity versus v -1/2 for the WN@BCN-900 electrode. Clearly, the plot can be divided into two regions according to the slope. When the sweep rates is slower than 5 mV s−1 (Region 1), the decrease of capacity is not significant as the increasing of sweep rates, indicating the rate-independent step of charge transfer [38,60]. In contrast, as the sweep rates over 5 mV s−1 (Region 2), the capacity sharply decreases, suggesting that the lithium-storage at this region is a rate-limiting diffusion process. The limitation of capability at fast sweep rates is attributed to the increased resistance of the active materials and diffusion constrains [58,61]. In addition, the contribution of capacitance at a fixed potential can be quantitatively calculated by the equation (3) [62]:
(3)
i (V) =
k
1
v +
k 2
v
1/2
in which i(V), k 1 v and k 2 v 1/2 represent the current at a fixed potential, capacitive-controlled current and diffusion-controlled current, respectively.

The equation (3) can be easily rearranged as follows:
(4)
i (V) /
v
1/2
=
k
1
v
1/2
+
k 2

According to the line fitting of i(V)v 1/2–v 1/2 plot, the values of k 1 and k 2 can be obtained, which were used to calculate the part of capacitive contribution. Fig. 6e shows the calculated capacitive part of CV curve (grey region) and the original CV curve (blue line) at a sweep rate of 5 mV s−1. Based on the integral calculation, the capacitive contribution is 86.9% of the total lithium-storage. In contrast, the diffusion-controlled process is much less, which is mainly occurs around the peak potential. The contribution ratios of diffusion-controlled and capacitive processes at other sweep rates were also calculated as shown in Fig. 6f. With the increase of sweep rates, the contribution ratio of capacitive charge gradually increases from 51.2% at 0.1 mV s−1 to 86.9% at 5 mV s−1. The high ratio of capacitive behavior for lithium-storage guarantees the fast surface electrochemical reactions with little structural degradation of active materials, confirming the high-rate performance and long cycling stability.

A new approach to synthesize composite electrode materials with WN nanoparticles dispersed on porous BCN has been demonstrated. The composite nanomaterial exhibited excellent electrochemical properties used as anode for Li-ion batteries. One of the samples (WN@BCN-900) displayed superior rate capability and cycling stability. Results also show that the pseudocapacitive mechanism plays a dominating role in the lithiation/delithiation processes. The composite material described in this work holds a great promise for the development of high-performance LIBs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are grateful for the financial support from the National Natural Foundation of China (21703113 and 51801108), the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province, China (ZR2019YQ08), the Taishan Scholars Advantageous and Distinctive Discipline Program of Shandong Province and the World-Class University and Discipline of Shandong Province, the Foundation of Key Laboratory of Low-Carbon Conversion Science & Engineering, Shanghai Advanced Research Institute, Chinese Academy of Sciences (KLLCCSE-201904, SARI, CAS), Chongqing Key Laboratory of Photo-Electric Functional Material, and the Australian Research Council (ARC) is also acknowledged for supporting this study under the FL170100101 project. We give thanks to Dr. Na Ta in State Key Laboratory of Catalysis, Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences for the help in the testing of HAADF-STEM.
